title: Ser vs Estar
intro: Spanish has two verbs meaning 'to be': ser and estar. This is one of the most important things to learn — and easier than it looks!

sections.title: When to use SER
sections.content:
Use SER for things that are stable or inherent:
• Identity: Soy Ana. (I'm Ana.)
• Origin / nationality: Soy de México. Soy mexicana.
• Occupation: Es médico. (He's a doctor.)
• Time / dates: Son las tres. (It's three o'clock.) Hoy es lunes.
• Material: La mesa es de madera. (The table is made of wood.)
• Permanent traits: El elefante es grande. (Elephants are big.)

sections.title: When to use ESTAR
sections.content:
Use ESTAR for things that can change:
• Location: El banco está a la derecha. (The bank is on the right.)
• Temporary state: Estoy cansado. (I'm tired.)
• Feelings / mood: Está triste. (She's sad.)
• Result of a change: La puerta está abierta. (The door is open.)
• Progressive tense: Estoy comiendo. (I'm eating.)

sections.title: Side by side — spot the difference

items.es: El café es caliente. ❌  →  El café está caliente. ✓
items.en: The coffee is hot (temporary state → estar)

items.es: Madrid es en España. ❌  →  Madrid está en España. ✓

items.note: ser for job, estar for current state

sections.label: Memory trick — DOCTOR for SER
sections.content:
SER = Description (permanent) · Origin · Characteristics · Time · Occupation · Relationships
ESTAR = Position/Location · Action (progressive) · Condition · Emotion

questions.explanation: Occupation is permanent → SER → Soy profesora ✓
Tiredness is temporary → ESTAR → Estoy cansado ✓
Nationality is permanent → SER → Es española ✓
Location → ESTAR → El banco está a la derecha ✓
Days and dates → SER → Hoy es lunes ✓
Result of a change (open/closed can change) → ESTAR → La puerta está abierta ✓
Height is a permanent physical trait → SER → Es alto ✓
